findings.excerpt: The standards established by this organization are also called "ISO".
findings.note: Slight tone shift from JP source ('呼んだりします' is colloquial). The English is grammatical and natural — but the Japanese source phrases it more loosely ('also sometimes called'). Reader-side: no stumble, mild flattening only.

findings.excerpt: "Venture" means "adventure" and "Capital" means "capital".
findings.note: Reads as tautology to an EN reader ('Capital means capital'). Not wrong, since the JP source explicitly glosses 「資本」, but EN readers will find it odd / circular. Consider 'Capital means funds/financial resources'.

findings.excerpt: four perspectives: 'Financial,' 'Customer,' 'Business Process,' and 'Learning and Growth.'
findings.note: Uses single quotes with comma-inside-quote (American style). Mixed within the corpus — other entities use double quotes. Consistency issue across pages, not blocking on this single page.

findings.excerpt: By providing 'training data' to a computer, the computer itself becomes capable of making future predictions and decisions.
findings.note: Single quotes around 'training data'; on p146 term::p146::2 the same phrase is rendered as 'learning data' (no quotes inconsistency, but lexical inconsistency).

findings.excerpt: table: '"training data"' vs term: '"learning data"' for the same JP source 「学習用のデータ」
findings.note: Same page, same Japanese phrase translated two different ways within neighboring entities. 'Training data' is the EN-standard ML term; 'learning data' is a literal calque. Reader sees the inconsistency immediately.

findings.excerpt: A model that layers neural networks into multiple layers
findings.note: Redundant phrasing — 'layers ... into multiple layers'. Native-EN reader stumbles. Consider 'A multi-layer neural network model' or 'A model that stacks neural networks into multiple layers'.

findings.excerpt: One form of machine learning
findings.note: Missing terminal period. Inconsistent with sister entity term::p146::2 which ends with a period. Several entities on this page omit the final period after the second sentence.

findings.excerpt: A factory that improves efficiency by connecting factory machines to a network. Also called a "smart factory"
findings.note: Defining 'Smart Factory' as 'Also called a smart factory' is a tautology for an EN reader — the JP literally says スマート工場 (smart kōjō) which IS the alternate name, but the EN rendering 'smart factory' is the SAME as the headword 'Smart Factory'. Reader confusion.

findings.excerpt: Which is an appropriate example of Freemium?
findings.note: Term 'フリーミアム' (Freemium) is referenced bare in EN; an undergraduate EN reader will likely know the term, but a brief inline gloss ('a free-tier-plus-paid-upgrade business model') would help. Not blocking.

findings.excerpt: A. A fast-food restaurant service where, if you hold a purchased flat-rate pass, you can receive unlimited drinks within the validity period
findings.note: Long, comma-heavy sentence with embedded conditional. Reads more like a contract clause than natural English. Could be tightened: 'A fast-food service that lets you drink unlimited beverages within the validity period of a flat-rate pass you purchased.'

findings.excerpt: Unlike Hosting Service, the location of the contracted server is unclear.
findings.note: 'Hosting Service' is capitalized as if a defined proper-noun term, but no anchor exists on this entity (the comparison panels are in a SEPARATE entity figure::p199::2 below). EN reader reading just term::p199::0 has no referent. Should be 'Unlike a hosting service,' (lowercase, indefinite).

findings.excerpt: The customer essentially rents the installation site.
findings.note: 'Essentially' is an informal hedge in an otherwise formal definition register. The JP uses 「〜イメージ」 (image/concept). Better: 'The customer effectively rents only the installation site.'

findings.excerpt: Cannot find errors in the program
findings.note: This is the 'Disadvantage of White Box Test' cell. The original JP says 「プログラムの誤りを見つけられない」 in context of a white-box test column header — but stated as a flat 'cannot find errors' it CONTRADICTS the entire purpose of testing. EN reader will be very confused: white-box tests are known specifically for finding errors. The JP source itself is suspect / mis-OCR'd; the EN translator passed it through verbatim. This is a high-impact reader stumble.

findings.excerpt: Since not all complex tests are performed, there is a possibility that low-frequency defects (Bug) may not be found
findings.note: Awkward parenthetical '(Bug)' — singular, capitalized, with no article. Reads as a label rather than a gloss. Better: '(bugs)' lowercase plural, or remove entirely. Also 'all complex tests' is ambiguous — meant 'all possible test combinations.'

findings.excerpt: New Sales Management System Operations Service Level Agreement (SLA) Version 2.0 6/28/2020 TekuTeku Co., Ltd.
findings.note: Caption is a run-on string with no separators. 'New Sales Management System Operations' / 'Service Level Agreement (SLA)' / 'Version 2.0' / '6/28/2020' / 'TekuTeku Co., Ltd.' should be separated by punctuation. Also date '6/28/2020' is US-style but the rest of the corpus uses Japanese-style dates (令和2年 etc.). Reader cannot parse this as continuous prose.

findings.excerpt: When you specify an argument when calling a function, that argument is passed to the function.
findings.note: This is a 'term' entity, but the 'surface' (headword) is a full sentence, not a term. This is a Stage-5/structural defect surfaced through the EN reader's eye: the entity-type is wrong (should be type:paragraph or type:explanation, not type:term). Reader sees a 'glossary entry' whose headword is a sentence — extremely confusing.

findings.excerpt: Program example: Function call (with arguments) — ○hello(string: name) / output name / output "Konnichiwa" / hello("Yamada-san")
findings.note: Mixed-language artifact: 'Konnichiwa' is romaji (transliteration) of こんにちは; 'Yamada-san' uses Japanese honorific -san without gloss. Per spec, transliterated proper nouns are OK, but 'Konnichiwa' is a common-noun greeting, not a proper noun — better translated to 'Hello' with an explanatory note, or kept as こんにちは and explained. Also the figure's '○' marker bullet is undocumented in EN convention.

findings.excerpt: Output example: Yamada-san Konnichiwa
findings.note: The EN 'output example' contains untranslated romaji ('Konnichiwa') and an unglossed Japanese honorific ('-san'). EN reader unfamiliar with Japanese cannot map this to the lecture content. Acceptable IF the output of the program is verbatim Japanese (it is — the program outputs こんにちは); but then it should be in original kana, not romaji.

findings.excerpt: The Boundaries That Distinguish Computers Are Becoming Blurry
findings.note: Like page 363, this 'term' headword is actually a full sentence (in Title Case). EN reader expecting a glossary entry encounters a thesis statement. Entity-type should be paragraph/note, not term.

findings.excerpt: There are many types of "computers". ... Today, the criteria used to distinguish types of computers are essentially only "size" and "processing power".
findings.note: Heavy use of double-quoted terms inside flowing prose. Acceptable, but density is high — 3+ quoted strings in a 2-sentence paragraph slows reading.

findings.excerpt: In the IT Passport exam, it is sometimes asked with the phrase "a protocol for viewing email managed on a mail server."
findings.note: 'It is sometimes asked with the phrase' — slight literal-translation feel. More natural: 'In the IT Passport exam, this is sometimes phrased as ...'. Not blocking.

findings.excerpt: Relationship between address and IP Address
findings.note: Inconsistent capitalization within one phrase: lowercase 'address' on first occurrence, Title Case 'IP Address' on second (in same title). Both refer to the same concept; the JP source uses 「住所」 (=address, real-world postal address) vs 「IPアドレス」 (=IP address), but the EN distinction is lost AND the casing is inconsistent. Reader pauses to figure out if these are two different concepts.

findings.excerpt: must be a unique value that does not duplicate anywhere in the world.
findings.note: 'Does not duplicate' is intransitive use of 'duplicate' — slightly awkward. Better: 'is unique worldwide' or 'has no duplicates anywhere in the world'.

findings.excerpt: A. Configure MAC address filtering. ... B. Stop ESSID notifications ...
findings.note: Markers use 'A.' / 'B.' / 'C.' / 'D.' consistently — good. No duplicate prefix bug ('A. Option A.') observed on this page. Marker style aligns across all question pages reviewed.

cross_page_observations.marker_consistency: A./B./C./D. used consistently across all question pages (p181, p300, p555). No 'D. Option D' duplicate-marker collision observed.
cross_page_observations.quoting_style: Mixed single-quote vs double-quote usage across entities; not within a single entity. Low-severity but visible.
cross_page_observations.untranslated_residuals: p363 contains romaji ('Konnichiwa', 'Yamada-san') in code examples — flagged WARN. No CJK characters leaked into EN strings on any other page reviewed.
cross_page_observations.title_case: Generally consistent Title Case for term.surface.en (e.g., 'Trademark Right', 'Cloud Computing'). p392 and p363 violate this by putting full sentences in the surface field.
cross_page_observations.date_format: p292 uses '6/28/2020' (US m/d/y) — inconsistent with the rest of the corpus's Japanese-era / kanji dates. Single occurrence.

blockers: p249 row 'Cannot find errors in the program' — semantic contradiction; EN reader will be confused (FAIL)
p363 term::p363::3 — sentence-as-headword in a 'term' entity (FAIL)
recommendation: Fix the two FAIL items in iter_5 follow-up. WARN items are surface-level polish — acceptable for v1.0.0 release but worth a pass in v1.0.1. INFO items are stylistic.
